blueprints.pageSubtitle: View, download, replace or delete native and community blueprints.
blueprints.uploadHint: Pick a .blueprint.json file. It is validated against the official schema before becoming active.
blueprints.uploadInvalidJson: The file is not valid JSON.
blueprints.replaceIdMismatch: Uploaded file does not carry id "{{expected}}". When replacing, the meta.id values must match.
blueprints.deleteConfirmBody: Really delete blueprint "{{name}}" ({{id}})? Existing servers are not affected, but new server instances using this blueprint will no longer be possible.

docs.pageSubtitle: Create, upload and configure server types
docs.manageBlueprintsHint: Upload, replace and delete happen on the "Blueprints" page.

intro.title: What is a Blueprint?
intro.body: A Blueprint is a pure JSON definition for a server type — e.g. "DayZ server" or "Minecraft server", not a running server itself. It tells the panel which Docker image to use, which ports the server needs, how it starts and where the server files come from (Steam, HTTPS download, ready-made image or manual). Any number of server instances can be created from one Blueprint. Blueprints never contain scripts or hooks — they are strictly validated before they touch any container.

workflow.title: The MSM workflow
workflow.body:
How MSM is used top-down:

1. Pick a server type (Blueprint) or create and upload your own.
2. Click "Create Server" on the Servers page and choose the Blueprint in the game dropdown.
3. Enter name, host IP, ports and limits (CPU/RAM/Disk).
4. Install the server (downloads game files via Steam/HTTPS) and start it.

From there MSM manages console, backups, mods, file manager and logs — the Blueprint is just the template.

addServer.title: Add a server (instance)
addServer.body:
If a matching Blueprint already exists:

1. Open "Servers" in the sidebar.
2. Click "Create Server".
3. Pick a game — the list is built from registered Blueprints (native + community).
4. Choose a name and host IP. Must be a concrete interface, never 0.0.0.0 (otherwise Docker would bypass the UFW rules).
5. Optionally enter game/query/RCon ports or leave "auto".
6. Set CPU/RAM/Disk limits (empty = unlimited).
7. Create the server and click "Install" on the detail page.

If the game is not on Steam, MSM downloads the build from the HTTPS link defined in the Blueprint.

addBlueprintSteam.title: Create a Blueprint: Steam game
addBlueprintSteam.body:
You need the Steam AppID of the dedicated server (look it up on SteamDB).

1. Click "Download Blueprint Template" above and open the file.
2. Fill in:
   • meta.id — a short slug like "my-rust". Must not collide with a native Blueprint.
   • meta.name / meta.description — display values in the game dropdown.
   • runtime.image — matching Docker image (e.g. a steamcmd-based image).
   • runtime.startup — argv template, e.g. "./RustDedicated +server.port {GAME_PORT} +server.queryport {QUERY_PORT}".
   • ports — declare the roles the server uses (game/query/rcon, each tcp or udp).
   • source.type = "steam"; set source.steam.appId, platform (linux/windows) and compatibility (native/proton).
   • Optional mods: supportsSteamWorkshop=true, workshopAppId, modInjection.
3. Save the file as <id>.blueprint.json (filename must match meta.id exactly).
4. Click "Upload Blueprint" below — the Blueprint appears in the game dropdown immediately.

addBlueprintCustom.title: Create a Blueprint: non-Steam (HTTP / Docker / custom)
addBlueprintCustom.body:
For games not on Steam or with a custom build, source.type decides how MSM installs the server:

• HTTP source (build hosted as ZIP/tarball on an HTTPS server):
  source.type = "http", source.http.url = HTTPS URL, optionally source.http.sha256 for integrity. MSM streams the archive straight into the install directory (5 GiB hard cap, Zip-Slip and Zip-Bomb protection).

• Docker-only (everything is already in the image):
  source.type = "dockerOnly". No install step — the image is started directly.

• Custom (manual install):
  source.type = "custom". You must place the server files into the install directory yourself (e.g. via the File Manager) before starting.

runtime, ports and (optional) mods are filled in the same way as a Steam Blueprint. Save the file as <id>.blueprint.json and click "Upload Blueprint".

location.title: Where do Blueprints live?
location.body:
Native Blueprints ship with the panel (read-only, in the repository).
Community Blueprints live under /opt/msm/blueprints/community/ and survive panel updates.
Filenames follow <id>.blueprint.json and must match meta.id exactly.

schema.title: JSON schema at a glance
schema.body:
Every Blueprint shares the same top-level objects. What each one does:

• meta — id, name, category, author, description. Drives display and filtering in the panel.
• runtime — Docker image, startup template (argv) and environment variables for the container.
• ports — list of role (game/query/rcon/voice/web/custom) and protocol (tcp/udp). Actual port numbers are chosen when the server is created.
• source — where the server data comes from: steam, http, dockerOnly or custom.
• mods (optional) — unlocks the Workshop/mod UI and decides how mods are passed to the server.

runtime.title: runtime — image, startup, env
runtime.body:
runtime.image: the Docker image MSM runs in the container.
runtime.env: extra environment variables, usable as {ENV.NAME} in the startup line.
runtime.startup: an argv template. The renderer tokenizes the string first with shlex.split, then substitutes whitelisted tokens only:

• {GAME_PORT}, {QUERY_PORT}, {RCON_PORT}
• {INSTALL_DIR}, {MOD_ARG}
• {ENV.MY_VAR} for custom variables from runtime.env

Shell substitution ($, backticks, $(...) and ${...}) is rejected at validation time. Mod IDs containing semicolons can never spawn new argv elements — they stay literal arguments.

ports.title: ports — roles, not numbers
ports.body:
Blueprints only declare port roles, not concrete numbers. When a server is created the UI renders one input per declared role, where the actual port number is set.

Roles: game, query, rcon, voice, web, custom.
Protocols: tcp, udp.

source.title: source — where does the server come from?
source.body:
Pick exactly one strategy:

• steam — appId, platform (linux/windows), compatibility (native/proton). MSM downloads the server via SteamCMD.
• http — HTTPS URL plus optional sha256 for integrity. The archive is streamed into the install directory.
• dockerOnly — the Docker image already contains the server, no separate install step.
• custom — manual install. You must place the server data into the install directory yourself before starting.

httpSecurity.title: HTTP source security
httpSecurity.body:
HTTP downloads are HTTPS-only and stream the archive into the install directory (5 GiB hard cap). The downloader

• rejects redirects to private/loopback IPs (SSRF guard),
• refuses symlink/hardlink members,
• validates every member path against the install directory (Zip-Slip guard),
• enforces a 10 GiB decompressed cap with a 1:100 per-member ratio (Zip-/Tar-Bomb guard).

Set source.http.sha256 to enforce content integrity — recommended for any external download.

mods.title: mods & Steam Workshop
mods.body:
supportsSteamWorkshop=true unlocks the Workshop UI for this server type and requires workshopAppId.

modInjection picks how the panel passes mods to the server:

• none — mod system off.
• startupArg — modStartupArgumentFormat is added as an argv element to the startup command.
• file — the workshop modlist is written to modListFilePath. The path is validated against install_dir so it can never escape.

import.title: Upload, replace or delete a Blueprint
import.body:
Owners or accounts with panel.settings.write manage Blueprints on the "Blueprints" page in the sidebar:

• Upload — drop in a new .blueprint.json. Validated against the same schema as native Blueprints.
• Replace — overwrite community Blueprints. The uploaded file must carry the same meta.id.
• Delete — remove community Blueprints. Native Blueprints are hard-protected and can be neither replaced nor deleted.

Alternatively via API: POST /api/blueprints/import (create + update) and DELETE /api/blueprints/{id} with a CSRF token. After a successful import the Blueprint appears immediately in the Create-Server game dropdown.

exampleMinecraftVersion.title: Example: change the Minecraft version
exampleMinecraftVersion.body:
Native Minecraft Blueprints are read-only (version "LATEST"). To pin a specific version, clone the Blueprint once as a community Blueprint:

1. On the "Blueprints" page, download the native variant you want (e.g. minecraft_paper).
2. In the JSON, change only meta.id (a unique slug), meta.name and runtime.env.VERSION — leave everything else alone.
3. Save as <new-id>.blueprint.json and upload via "Blueprints → Upload new".

Valid VERSION values (for itzg/minecraft-server): LATEST, SNAPSHOT, or a concrete version like 1.20.4. TYPE picks the server variant (VANILLA, PAPER, SPIGOT, PURPUR, FABRIC, FORGE, NEOFORGE, SPONGEVANILLA). Note: mods and plugins are not compatible across major versions — having one Blueprint per version makes that explicit.

exampleWine.title: Example: run a Windows server via Wine
exampleWine.body:
Some games only ship a Windows binary. For a Wine-based server, write a custom Blueprint with a dockerOnly source: an image that already contains Wine plus the server binaries (built by you — never a random image from the internet).

Things to watch:

• The Wine prefix (~/.wine) must be persistent — keep it in the bind mount under /data.
• No X server: only set DISPLAY=:0 if you actually run an Xvfb instance inside the image.
• Headless: the server binary must not open a GUI.
• Console input (e.g. EULA confirmation) works because MSM starts the container with --interactive — type directly in the console tab.

The example below starts a generic Windows server in a self-built Wine image. Adjust: meta.id, runtime.image (your registry), runtime.startup.

servers.subtitle: Manage your game servers
servers.createHint: Create your first server to get started.
servers.name: Name
servers.consoleClearTitle: Hide the current console output locally (server logs are preserved)
servers.confirmDelete: Really delete this server? All data will be permanently removed.
servers.publicBindIpHint: Required. Pick a concrete interface (no 0.0.0.0). Default: first public IP of the host.
servers.editNetworkDescription: Update bind IP and ports. For running servers the old firewall rules are removed, the container is recreated and started with the new bindings.

bindIp.hint: Choose from the available host interfaces. 0.0.0.0 is rejected so Docker cannot bypass UFW.
bindIp.startBlockedBody: The server can only start once a host IP is assigned. Without an explicit bind IP, Docker would bypass UFW (anti-leak).

disk.softLimitWarning: Disk usage hit {percent}% of the soft limit ({usedMb} MB / {limitMb} MB). Clean up old data — at 100% the server will be stopped automatically.
disk.softLimitStopped: Server was stopped due to disk soft limit ({usedMb} MB / {limitMb} MB).

roles.title: Roles
roles.subtitle: Manage global roles and permissions
roles.name: Name
roles.adminLocked: The admin role is hard-wired (all permissions). Not editable.
roles.userEditable: Default role for new users. Permissions are editable.

permissions.users.permissions.manage: Manage user roles and server permissions
permissions.server.view: View server in list and detail
permissions.server.config.write: Edit server settings (name, auto-restart, backup schedule)

serverPermissions.title: Permissions
serverPermissions.subtitle: Who can do what on this server
serverPermissions.revokeConfirm: Remove all permissions for this user on this server?
serverPermissions.ownerHint: Owner always has all permissions. Admin role has all permissions blanket.
serverPermissions.adminSubtitle: Delegate server permissions to individual sub-users. Pick a server first.
